retrieved_docs.title: Craniotomy -- Indications -- Parasitic lesions
retrieved_docs.content: Hydatid cyst, Racemose neurocysticercosis (NCC)

retrieved_docs.title: Tapeworm -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Neurocysticercosis (NCC) symptoms can range from asymptomatic brain lesions to mimicking any kind of neurological disorder. [3] Symptoms include seizures, focal neurological deficits, intracranial hypertension, cognitive decline, vague symptoms of a headache, associated stroke, or involuntary movements. Symptomatology often depends on the location of the brain lesions; for example, intraparenchymal cysts more often leading to seizures and headache, while cysts in the subarachnoid spaces and ventricles lead to hydrocephalus. [4] NCC is commonly associated with seizures and epilepsy; however, the risk of developing epilepsy is unclear. Many people with NCC may develop epilepsy, but the rates are highly variable and can be anywhere from 10% to 50%. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Tapeworm -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Since cysts in most tissues are asymptomatic or destroyed, they are rarely noticed or diagnosed. However, NCC leads to visible cystic lesions in the CNS and significant and variable symptomatology. A large part of NCC pathology is due to the host immune response to the T. solium larval cyst. [9] Once the cyst is in the tissue, the host immune system induces granuloma formation and perilesional edema.  In the initial stage of NCC, there is a viable cyst with minimal pericystic inflammation. As the host immune response increases, the perilesional inflammation will kill the cyst, the fluid inside the cyst becomes dense, and the cyst shrinks until it is eventually cleared or replaced with residual calcification. [4] MRI and CT can identify calcific granulomatous “ring-enhancing” lesions found in the brain. [9] The diagnosis of NCC depends on radiologic suspicion and congruent interpretation of serology. Radiology can stage degeneration and the degree of inflammation but is also used to monitor the effectiveness of treatment. [3] Serology aids in the confirmation of the diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Fahr Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis -- 3. Infectious Diseases
retrieved_docs.content: Brucellosis: A study by Mousa AM et al. showed 9 (13.8%) out of 65 cases of CNS brucellosis (neuropsychiatric manifestations) had radiologic evidence of basal ganglia calcifications. Three cases had unilateral, 4 cases had bilaterally symmetric, and 2 cases had bilaterally asymmetric calcifications. Toxoplasmosis: The most commonly affected region (75% cases) is basal ganglia showing multiple ring-enhancing lesions with edema and sometimes calcifications. Neurocysticercosis: Larval cyst may sometimes occur in basal ganglia and gets calcified. HIV/AIDS: In a study done for reporting neuropathology in 52 HIV patients, three were found to have basal ganglia calcifications.

retrieved_docs.content: Amebic meningoencephalitis Aspiration syndromes Bronchiectasis imaging Constipation Constrictive pericarditis Encopresis Intestinal malrotation Intestinal volvulus Intussusception Leishmaniosis

retrieved_docs.title: Tapeworm -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: NCC treatment is based on the location of brain involvement and the number of cysts and may include symptomatic therapy (antiepileptics), antiparasitic therapy, or surgery. Often more than one is needed. Of note, antiparasitic drugs can also lead to temporary inflammation and increased symptoms; therefore, caution must be exercised. [3] One or two parenchymal lesions may be treated with short courses of albendazole and corticosteroids, while multiple lesions require combination albendazole and praziquantel with steroids. [11] Ventricular cysts are often surgically removed, and subarachnoid cysts may require longer courses of antiparasitic and anti-inflammatories. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Arachnoid Cysts -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Neurocysticercosis cysts in the CSF (ie, racemose neurocysticercosis) can have individual cysts that appear very similar to an arachnoid cyst, but these cysts are typically multiloculated. In cases of neurocysticercosis, there are very likely other findings indicating neurocysticercosis on brain imaging, in history, and by immunology workup. Usually, with neurocysticercosis, there are brain lesions with calcifications.

retrieved_docs.title: Naegleria Infection and Primary Amebic Meningoencephalitis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: N fowleri infection causes PAM, which is a rapidly progressive and often fatal disease. The infection leads to severe cerebral edema, hemorrhagic necrosis, and increased intracranial pressure, which can result in brain herniation and death within days of symptom onset. Other complications include seizures, cranial nerve dysfunction, hydrocephalus, and coma.

retrieved_docs.title: Tapeworm -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: NCC has a good prognosis with symptoms improving after treatment, but prognosis varies depending on the location and burden of disease. Subarachnoid and intraventricular cysts have significant morbidity and mortality while single parenchymal brain lesions have a high chance of survival with no seizure relapses. [3] Multiple cysts can be lethal or cause recurrent seizures.

retrieved_docs.title: Cerebral Amyloid Angiopathy -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Nontraumatic ICH includes: Hemorrhagic tumor Hemorrhagic transformation of an ischemic stroke Lobar extension of a hypertensive putaminal hemorrhage Arteriovenous malformation (AVM) Other imaging differential diagnosis includes: Hypertensive microangiopathy Hemorrhagic metastases Multiple cavernoma syndrome Diffuse axonal injury Radiation-induced vasculopathy Neurocysticercosis

retrieved_docs.title: Helminthiasis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Taeniasis may be asymptomatic in most of the cases. When present, it may be due to abdominal pain and abdominal distension for intestinal taeniasis. Neurocysticercosis may present with recurrent seizures, paresis, obstructive hydrocephalus, headache, features of intracranial hypertension, stroke, cognitive decline, and depression. [21] [34] [35] [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Tapeworm -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment and management of neurocysticercosis require a team-based healthcare approach. NCC will often require neurology and infectious disease consultation but depending on the location of the lesions may also incorporate neurosurgery evaluation. NCC can present in any patient population and can sometimes mimic preeclampsia in pregnant women. There have been several case studies of pregnant women with NCC presenting with seizures or headaches. [13] [Level 5] The care for these patients involves multiple specialties to include perinatologists, neurosurgery, infectious disease specialists, and nursing. The transition to discharge must also be considered to involve case management for potential psychosocial concerns. Because treatment is often very costly, this can have a significant impact on the patient’s financial wellbeing. Therefore, the management of NCC goes beyond an interprofessional approach between specialists, but also into the psychosocial care for patients and even into the public sector for disease prevention and patient education.

retrieved_docs.title: Albendazole -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with neuro-helminth infection may experience symptoms such as focal neurologic deficits, seizures, and headaches more frequently compared to other parasitic infections due to the destruction of helminthic larvae and cysts in the brain. They also may experience meningeal signs and increased intracranial pressure due to the local inflammatory reaction from parasite death. Mitigating these symptoms is possible with the coadministration of steroids and anticonvulsant medication.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 1018
retrieved_docs.content: The CT scan of the head that reveals a periventricular cyst is classified as a كيسة دماغية (brain cyst), and the brain (mozek) is identified as the anatomical site where such cysts can be found. The brain also serves as the finding site for a range of other pathological conditions, including اعتلال دماغي أيضي (metabolic brain disease) and انْضِغاطُ الدِّماغ (brain compression). In addition, the brain is the location of أوديما دماغية (cerebral edema), اعتلال دماغي (general brain disease), and اعتلال دماغي سمي (toxic brain disease). Encephalitis, including التهاب الدماغ الفيروسي المنقول بالمفصليات (arthropod‑borne viral encephalitis), occurs within the brain, underscoring its role as a primary site of inflammatory neurological processes. Beyond disease, the brain is the physiological substrate for essential biological processes such as myšlení (thinking) and the emotional experience of الشعور بالذنب (feeling guilt). Therapeutic interventions that directly target the brain, such as العلاج بالتخليج الكهربي (electrocoagulation therapy), are performed at the brain itself. Traumatic brain edema (Traumatisch hersenoedeem) is a specific form of أوديما دماغية, indicating that traumatic injury can lead to secondary cerebral swelling. Likewise, إصابة حادة في الدماغ (acute brain injury) is considered a subtype of اعتلال دماغي, linking severe acute insults to the broader category of brain disease. Collectively, these relationships illustrate that the brain functions as the central locus for a diverse spectrum of structural abnormalities, inflammatory conditions, metabolic and toxic disorders, cognitive and emotional processes, and both diagnostic and therapeutic procedures, highlighting its pivotal importance in clinical assessment and patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: and secondary swelling of brain tissue. Together, these interconnections emphasize the centrality of the brain in diverse pathological, diagnostic, and therapeutic contexts, and they underscore the importance of recognizing overlapping disease categories for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2226
retrieved_docs.content: Traumatisch hersenoedeem is a specific subtype of أوديما دماغية, meaning that traumatic brain edema is classified under the broader condition of cerebral edema. As a form of أوديما دماغية, Traumatisch hersenoedeem involves swelling of brain tissue that results from head injury, leading to increased intracranial pressure and potential neurological impairment. Recognizing Traumatisch hersenoedeem as a subset of أوديما دماغية helps clinicians understand its pathophysiology within the context of general cerebral edema, guiding appropriate diagnostic evaluation and targeted therapeutic interventions to reduce brain swelling and protect neurological function.

The brain is the locus of higher cognitive functions such as thinking (myšlení) and emotional experiences like guilt (الشعور بالذنب), and it is the site of diverse pathologies including toxic brain disorder (اعتلال دماغي سمي), arthropod‑transmitted viral encephalitis (التهاب الدماغ الفيروسي المنقول بالمفصليات), and acute brain injury (إصابة حادة في الدماغ), which is a specific form of cerebral pathology (اعتلال دماغي). Vascular and cerebrovascular disorders intersect with these entities: cerebral artery sclerosis (تصلب الشرايين المخية) belongs to the physical‑psychosomatic subcluster and contributes to multiple lacunar infarcts (خَرَف احتشائي متعدّد); migraine with

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2894
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral edema (تكثر الدبق الدماغي) may be a finding of disease that presents with headache (صداع), indicating that increased intracranial pressure or brain swelling can manifest as head pain. In addition, lumbar puncture (البزل القطني (LP)) is clinically associated with headache, reflecting the common post‑procedural symptom that patients may experience after cerebrospinal fluid sampling. Together, these connections underscore that headache can arise both from underlying intracranial pathology such as cerebral edema and from iatrogenic causes like lumbar puncture, highlighting the need for careful assessment of headache etiology in clinical practice.

The biological process myšlení (thinking) is likewise linked to mozek, as is the emotional state الشعور بالذنب (feeling of guilt). Collectively, these relationships illustrate that the allowed qualifier unifies diagnostic imaging, surgical planning, diagnostic evaluation, and parasitological investigation across a wide spectrum of endocrine, metabolic, renal, ophthalmic, obstetric, cardiovascular, neurological, and infectious conditions, guiding comprehensive patient assessment and interdisciplinary care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2404
retrieved_docs.content: Encephalitis is a condition that occurs in the brain (mozek). The brain (mozek) also serves as the anatomical site for several pathological findings, including metabolic brain disorder (اعتلال دماغي أيضي), brain compression (انْضِغاطُ الدِّماغ), and toxic brain disorder (اعتلال دماغي سمي). These relationships highlight that the brain is both the location of encephalitis and the locus where these specific brain injuries or dysfunctions are identified, underscoring the importance of evaluating the brain for multiple concurrent or related pathologies in clinical assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1085
retrieved_docs.content: The CT scan of head showing a periventricular cyst is classified as a كيسة دماغية (brain cyst). This كيسة دماغية is located within the mozek (brain). Encephalitis is also described as occurring in the mozek. Within the mozek, additional findings may include اعتلال دماغي أيضي (metabolic brain disorder) and انْضِغاطُ الدِّماغ (brain compression). Anatomical locations associated with the mozek encompass أوديما دماغية (cerebral edema), اعتلال دماغي (brain lesion), and اعتلال دماغي سمي (toxic brain lesion). Traumatisch hersenoedeem (traumatic brain edema) is identified as a parent condition of أوديما دماغية, indicating that traumatic edema is a specific form of cerebral edema. Finally, إصابة حادة في الدماغ (acute brain injury) is recognized as a subtype of اعتلال دماغي, linking acute injury to broader brain pathology. This network of relationships highlights how imaging findings, infectious processes, metabolic disturbances, compressive forces, and traumatic or acute injuries interrelate within the brain, informing diagnosis and management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2397
retrieved_docs.content: The brain (mozek) is the anatomical location for a range of pathological findings and a key physiological process. Encephalitis is located in the brain. The brain serves as the finding site for a brain cyst (كيسة دماغية), a metabolic brain disorder (اعتلال دماغي أيضي), brain compression (انْضِغاطُ الدِّماغ), and a toxic brain disorder (اعتلال دماغي سمي). In addition, the brain is the site of the biological process of thinking (myšlení). This underscores the brain’s central role in both disease manifestation and cognitive function, highlighting its importance in medical evaluation and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 998
retrieved_docs.content: The brain (mozek) serves as the primary anatomical site for a range of neurological findings and processes. It is the location where a brain cyst (كيسة دماغية) may be identified, and it also harbors metabolic brain disease (اعتلال دماغي أيضي), brain compression (انْضِغاطُ الدِّماغ), and toxic brain disease (اعتلال دماغي سمي). Encephalitis is specifically described as occurring in the brain (location_of). More generally, the brain is the site of brain disease (اعتلال دماغي), and acute brain injury (إصابة حادة في الدماغ) is classified as a particular type of brain disease. Beyond pathology, the brain is the anatomical substrate for the biological process of thinking (myšlení). Together, these relationships underscore the central role of the brain in both disease manifestation and normal cognitive function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 175
retrieved_docs.content: CT scan of head: periventricular cyst is identified as a كيسة دماغية (brain cyst) and the finding is located in the mozek (brain). The mozek serves as the site for multiple pathological and radiological findings, including كيسة دماغية, اعتلال دماغي أيضي (metabolic brain disease), انْضِغاطُ الدِّماغ (brain compression), أوديما دماغية (brain edema), اضطراب دماغي غير محدد (unspecified brain disorder), اعتلال دماغي (brain pathology), and اعتلال دماغي سمي (toxic brain pathology). Encephalitis is also described as occurring in the mozek. The mozek is the anatomical location of the biological process myšlení (thinking) and is associated with the emotional experience الشعور بالذنب (feeling of guilt). Electrical ablation therapy (العلاج بالتخليج الكهربي) is performed directly on the mozek. Traumatisch hersenoedeem (traumatic brain edema) is a subtype of أوديما دماغية, indicating that traumatic swelling of brain tissue falls under the broader category of brain edema. Acute cerebrovascular disease is linked to persistent migraine aura with cerebral infarction, suggesting that acute vascular events can manifest as this specific migraine complication. Migraine with ischemic complication is classified both as اضطراب دماغي غير محدد and as persistent migraine aura with cerebral infarction, reinforcing its role as a distinct ischemic migraine presentation. A CT scan showing an enlarged Sylvian fissure (ct scan head enlarged sylvian fissure) is likewise categorized as اضطراب دماغي غير محدد. In the context of head trauma, إصابة رياضية (sports injury) is related to الارتجاج المخي (concussion), and اضطراب دماغي غير محدد is also related to الارتجاج المخي, highlighting the connection between traumatic events and various unspecified brain disorders. Finally, إصابة حادة في الدماغ (acute brain injury) is identified as a form of اعتلال دماغي, underscoring that severe acute injuries are encompassed within the broader spectrum of brain pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2297
retrieved_docs.content: Other nervous system disorders represent a broad category of neurological conditions, and within this category the specific condition خثار الجيب الوريدي الدماغي (cerebral venous sinus thrombosis) is considered a narrower, more specialized disorder. This relationship indicates that خثار الجيب الوريدي الدماغي is a particular manifestation of the wider group of other nervous system disorders.
